display_name: ARP incomplete entries do not always mean the host is down — check VLAN, subnet, proxy-ARP, and VRF assignment
context: ARP table shows 'Incomplete' for a destination IP, and the operator cannot reach the host from the network device
observation: The ARP entry for a directly connected host shows Incomplete (no MAC address resolved); pings to the host time out from the network device
naive_interpretation: The host is powered off, disconnected, or has a NIC failure — it is not responding to ARP requests
correct_interpretation: An Incomplete ARP entry means the device sent an ARP request and received no ARP reply within the timeout. While a dead or disconnected host is one possibility, there are several common network-side causes that produce the same symptom: (1) The host is in a different VLAN than the device's SVI — the ARP request never reaches the host because they are in different broadcast domains. (2) The host's IP is in a different subnet than the device's interface — the device sends ARP on its directly-connected interface, but the host's IP is not in that subnet's range, so the ARP broadcast goes to the wrong segment. (3) Proxy-ARP is disabled on the gateway — hosts in a different subnet that rely on proxy-ARP to reach the device will not get ARP replies. (4) The host is in a different VRF — the ARP request is sent in one VRF but the host's interface is in another. (5) A firewall or port security rule is dropping ARP frames. (6) Duplicate IP — another device answered first and the host's gratuitous ARP was suppressed. Always verify Layer 2 reachability (same VLAN, same broadcast domain) before concluding the host is down.
why: ARP operates at Layer 2 — it requires the source and destination to be in the same broadcast domain (VLAN); if they are not, the ARP request never reaches the host regardless of the host's state
Many operators configure SVIs and access ports independently, creating opportunities for VLAN mismatch — the SVI is in VLAN 100 but the host's port is in VLAN 200

Proxy-ARP is enabled by default on many Cisco platforms but disabled by default on others (SONiC, Junos, some EOS profiles) — disabling it silently breaks hosts that rely on it for default-gateway behavior in mismatched subnets
In VRF-lite or EVPN VXLAN environments, the ARP request may be sourced from a VRF that does not have the host's interface — the ARP goes out the wrong L3 context entirely

confirmation_commands.purpose: Check the ARP entry state and which interface/VLAN the ARP request was sent on
Verify the ARP state and the interface where the device expects to reach this IP
If the host MAC is known from another source, check whether it appears in the MAC table and on which VLAN/port — a VLAN mismatch will be visible here
Verify whether proxy-ARP is enabled on the gateway interface — if disabled, hosts in mismatched subnets will not get ARP replies
Display ARP table entries and their interface bindings to verify the ARP request is going out the correct interface and VLAN
Display ARP entries without DNS resolution to check for incomplete or failed entries and their associated interfaces

traps: Immediately blaming the host or its NIC when the real problem is a VLAN or subnet mismatch on the network side
Not checking which VLAN the SVI is in vs which VLAN the host's access port is in — they must match for ARP to work
Assuming proxy-ARP is enabled when it was explicitly or implicitly disabled — this breaks any host that relies on it for cross-subnet communication

In EVPN/VXLAN fabrics, forgetting that ARP suppression may be answering on behalf of remote hosts — an incomplete entry in this context may mean the EVPN control plane has not learned the host's MAC yet, not that the host is unreachable
Not considering duplicate IP addresses — if two hosts claim the same IP, ARP behavior becomes unpredictable and one of them will show stale or incomplete entries
